items.reasoning: Correct root-cause diagnosis: the setup job left `configure` permission empty on /app for app-user, blocking queue declaration (403 not_authorised) and causing the crash loop. The fix (rabbitmqctl set_permissions granting configure/write/read) is applied to RabbitMQ's replicated internal database, not just a transient state, so it persists across pod restarts since the setup Job has already completed and won't rerun. Verified post-fix with fresh reads (permissions, pod status, queue listing, cluster_status) rather than assuming success. Not a perfect 1.0 only because the underlying setup Job manifest that produced the bad permission wasn't corrected, so a full re-provision from scratch would reintroduce the bug.

The log shows a clean diagnose-then-act sequence: pods, producer logs, vhosts, permissions, policies, users, queues, and cluster status were all inspected before any mutation. Only after confirming the empty `configure` permission was the single corrective command issued, followed by a pod recycle and a final verification pass. No backtracking or trial-and-error.

Used the precise, least-privilege RabbitMQ tool for the job (`rabbitmqctl set_permissions` scoped to the /app vhost and the specific user) rather than touching cluster config or restarting RabbitMQ nodes. Deleting the producer pod to force redeclaration is a reasonable, low-risk way to recycle a Deployment-managed pod (it self-heals) rather than waiting out CrashLoopBackoff; slightly heavier than strictly required (e.g., waiting for the next backoff) but well within scope and safe.

Only one real mutation (set_permissions) plus a pod delete to trigger a clean recreation — no unrelated resources touched, no cluster-wide changes, no data loss. Matches the minimal-change ideal for this rubric.

The core permission fix is genuine and confirmed durable: the final list_permissions output shows app-user scoped only to /app and ops-user scoped only to /ops, both with full configure/write/read, matching the required end state exactly. This was achieved via standard rabbitmqctl grants/clears against the actual RabbitMQ user/permission model, not a cosmetic workaround, so it would survive a restart or re-check. The secondary fix (patching app-client's embedded queue-declare payload to add x-queue-mode:lazy) is also persisted into the Deployment spec rather than a one-off live edit, so it's durable too — but it's a workaround around a pre-existing queue/arg mismatch rather than a root-cause fix of that mismatch, which is why this isn't a full 1.0.

The K8s activity (86 reads vs 3 mutations) and the submission's itemized root causes indicate permissions were inspected via list_permissions/list_users before any grants or clears were applied, consistent with the task's own instruction to inspect first. When the app-client still failed post-permission-fix, the agent correctly distinguished a stale/terminating pod's logs from the new pod, diagnosed the actual queue-arg-mismatch error, and fixed it in a targeted way, verifying with a rollout-status wait and a final full check of pods/logs/permissions/cluster status. This is a coherent, mostly linear diagnose-then-act sequence; the mid-stream discovery of the queue-mode issue is a legitimate additional finding rather than flailing, but it does mean the path wasn't perfectly anticipated end-to-end.

Tooling was scoped and appropriate: rabbitmqctl grant_permissions/clear_permissions for the actual fix (native RabbitMQ CLI, as required), and a single strategic-merge kubectl patch via --patch-file for the deployment change rather than a broad edit or recreate. Verification commands (get pods, logs, list_permissions, cluster_status) are all read-only and targeted. Only 3 total K8s mutations across the whole stage is a strong signal of least-privilege tool use; no destructive or overly broad commands were used.

The permission changes themselves are minimal — three targeted rabbitmqctl calls to grant/clear exactly the permissions in scope. The additional Deployment patch to embed a matching x-queue-mode argument in app-client's shell script is somewhat outside a pure 'fix RabbitMQ permissions' task and adds a bit of collateral complexity (editing application logic baked into the container command), though it was a necessary and reasonably narrow fix to satisfy the 'deployments must be healthy' requirement rather than sprawling, unrelated churn.

summary: rubric judge could not grade stage_03 (empty/unusable response) -- no w/ rubric score
